sentences.text: Oh, yes.
It was after they had fled Dijon and they were seeking refuge in a small place called Yenne, Y-E-N-N-E.
It is above [NON-ENGLISH].
It's-- it's rather nice, but at the time, it was not touristic yet.
And they lived in-- it was an annex, you could say of some sort of barn.
I remember hay in the back and very small windows, like you have in barns.
And Mrs.
Oppenheimer wore an apron, the way the German women wear.
And on the trunks, and on the few pieces, crates and things, she had put embroidered doilies.
And she had put little curtains with ruffles at that barn window.
And you know, it broke my heart.
A farmer there.
They were hiding.
Oh, no.
It was rather easy.

You could absolutely travel with train, with bike, with other arrangement, with cars, because the zone of demarcation no longer existed between the occupied France and non-occupied France, so-called non-occupied.
I always kept writing to them.
They got the letters.
No.
It was the name of the farmer.
And I wrote to them often enough, maybe once a week, to help, to help them.
They felt good about it, you know?
And when the summer came, I went to see them.
I took my bike, and oh, that was probably about 75 miles.
But I did it in one day.
Not bad, you know?
I had good legs.
And I slept, I don't know, at the little inn there, that-- nothing.
Anyway, I remained about a week with them.
And the grandfather was dying.
I knew he wouldn't-- and the children were not there-- Maurice, Dorothy, Anita-- they had been dispersed someplace else.
I mean, families tried not to stay together.
At least one would escape-- and I think Dorothy did.
And Mr.
Oppenheimer took me for walks and he told me about his fears.
And he had no-- no hope of escaping.
He said that he was doomed.
And that was that.
They were running out of money.
I mean, how could I give them money?
I mean, I was 19 years old.
What about the farm?
Where they-- did somebody inform on them?
No, they were doing nothing.
They were older, they were living on their meager resources, selling the few things they had left, and eventually the-- the French police came to arrest them.
Probably somebody said something.
You know, most of them were arrested because somebody told.
Who told?
I know our maid who told on us, and my father fired her.
And we had another maid who came after the first man was lynched at the liberation-- she had a handkerchief, like in a ball.
And she had blood on it.
She said, I got blood from him.
My father fired her-- two minutes, she didn't last.
Oh, he was very adamant for these things.
Then I came to America.
My husband, well, it was difficult to find an apartment so we-- some people rented an apartment while they were in Florida, Jewish people named Pollock in the Bronx, near the Yankee Stadium.
And I didn't know any English.
I had learned the Greek, Latin, some Hebrew, and German, but I spoke in Yiddish.
I mean, all these old people were sitting on the benches.
They became my best friends.
And then the neighbor, Mrs.
Adler, saw my-- me and my husband, and she said, your wife doesn't know how to cook.
How could I know?
We had nothing to cook to begin with, and then we had a cook.
So I didn't know how to cook.
So she said, I'll teach your wife how to cook.
So she taught me how to make [NON-ENGLISH] matzo balls, and I thought it was American food.
[LAUGHS] Until I learned that, no, it was ethnic food.
And I had the children and then my husband died of a heart attack.
We were living in New Jersey.
And I remained alone with the children.
It was Thanksgiving Day, for which I give no thanks whatsoever, and I didn't know what to do.
He has three sisters, and the three sisters came, took him away, buried him according to the orthodox thing-- the same day, you know?
And they dropped me.
And my nephew said the rabbi said that my uncle died because he married you.
So that was-- it was racism-- reversed.
Well, you know, anyway, I understood.
I understood that after what had happened in Europe, they couldn't have warm feeling for me, and the children were not raised Jewish-- they were not circumcised.
And they were Christian.
And my husband understood.
He said you leave your country, you leave your language, you leave your family, you leave your culture, you cannot leave your religion, too.
He was-- he was a good man.
So I decided to go back home-- to go back to France with the children.
It was the time of Dien Bien Phu, the beginning of the problems in Algeria and the colonies.
Many of my friends lost their husbands in the colonial wars.

And I decided, after I found my room where I had been a young girl, it was not for me anymore And my husband was very American, very patriotic-- not the American Legion type, but he looked like the Roosevelts.
So I felt my children should be raised in America.
But how?
We had the life insurance.
I could live maybe five years.
And after that, what do I do?
My little boy was only three.
So I decided since I had a masters degree, I would become a teacher.
So I came back here in the summer, and I went to Teacher's College.
And I learned what it is to be an idiot at Teacher's College-- oh, my.
I mean, to think that they formed teachers there, that was shameful.
Anyway, I wrote such good paper that they sent me to the main university to get a Ph.D.
So in that, they were good.
And I told my parents, I have an occasion to do a Ph.D.
That's going to take much of my money, but can I leave the children with you?
And they said, all right.
So I left the children in France with my father and my mother for three years, except that I spend six months of each year for the summer vacation with them.
And I took all my classes, everything, in 18 months [LAUGHS]
I took my German, my Latin exam, boom, boom, boom, quick.
You know, I had a good French education.
That helped me a lot.
And then I landed my first job at Bonn University, where I finished my dissertation.
When I had my job, I had my children come back to me.

And I was there in competition with men who had nothing to do than to get home and put their feet under the table and eat dinner and do their research, where I had to care for family, to earn a living, having no maid, no help, no washing machine, taking care of the homework.
My actual work was my vacation.
And with that I managed to publish more than the whole faculty together in seven years, but they didn't give me tenure because I was a woman.

But anyway, I had seven good years where my children became accustomed to America in a smaller town, where a lot of French was spoken, with the Canadian-- and where they went to private school, because the public school were horrible.
And when they took-- and that it's one of my pet peeve with the system in America-- they took English right away, and they took French as second language.
When they entered school in September, they didn't know any English.
By the time Christmas came, they spoke fluently.
At the end of the year, they knew more than the others, because their schooling was more advanced in Europe than here.
And they took French as second language to learn the grammar, the spelling, and everything.
And that's what we should do with our Spanish-speaking people, instead of putting them in a cultural ghetto as we do.
Well, it was '42, '43, because we-- we had no food.
And you know, it kind of diminishes your spirit, you could say, where our aim of the day was to find a few potatoes, for instance.
We had little bags that had been sewn out of towels where we put our bread so my father would not eat our bread by mistake, or we wouldn't eat a crumb of somebody else.
The bread was the equivalent of two slices of pumpernickel for the day.
But butter ration was like one square of butter a month for four people.
I was in school.
Oh, sure.
He was working in the same thing.
My mother too.
I have to say for the French school system, that our curriculum never changed.
We had exactly the same curriculum that we had before the war, the same number of hours of Latin, the same of Greek, the same of chemistry, the same-- not like here.
When your money goes, the programs go.
No.
Well, they didn't make much money, but then you had to buy everything at the market, at the black market.
If your butter costs $30, if you're-- a piece of meat costs $50, I mean, your money doesn't go very far.
We have bought meat that was inedible.
We didn't know if it was human meat or-- or what.
We had to throw it away, you know?
It was that bad.
And we were hungry, and we were cold.
We had no heat.
And gas to cook the meal, we had only a few hours a day.
That's why hot water to me is a great thing in America.
Very much.
They are very sensitive to it.
They know Rita.

And they know not only that war, but they know the First World War because when we go to the countryside, you can see the monument to the death, and you see the name of a whole family-- five, six bothers killed.
You know, when you think of America as the population is now, making comparison, it is as if you had a war where you would lose around 15 million young men.
That's what the First World War cost us.
In France.
The First World War cost us a million and a half young men.
Compare the population of France then, and the loss-- it would be like 15 millions in America.
Oh, sure, because-- yeah.
And they know Rita very well.
And of course, because of their father, I certainly kept them well informed of what happened.
Yes, they know a lot about it.
No, not at all.
My first-- my older son, maybe-- he's much more sensitive to it.
My other son, no.
He had some bad-- bad contacts with Jewish kids, I don't know.
He's out of it.
But not my first son.
My first son is-- he's very much moved by all that.
Very--
Is Ronald.
The one that lives downstairs.
Yeah, I speak about Ronald.
But Robert was-- his father was three years old when he died, and he's extremely French.
I mean, you couldn't find more French than he.
He goes to France every year, he has a French program with students-- he's teaching French in the university, he's on French television.
I mean, he's French.
But not the other one.
[LAUGHS]
I would do better, I hope.
I hope.
If I have a general feeling, it's regret.
Not maybe risking my life more, because I don't know, but telling nice things, telling people I love them and kissing them, and hugging them, and being more with them.
But we were-- we are Protestant and we are really quite reserved.
My parents kissed me once a year, so I was not much given to that type of physical expression.
That's one thing I regret.
But otherwise, continuing my family life, my attitude with my parents, I don't think I could have done much more.
What I did, it was outside of them.
Oh, yes.
Well, mostly I dream of my young son, because he physically looks Jewish.
And when he was a little boy, and I was thinking of the mothers who had their children taken away from them, it's something that I can't bear.
And if I wake up at night, night is finished.
To Germany?
Yes, once, to see my friend Wolf Schieck.
My first sabbatical, I decided I'm going to see him.
He-- he was a professor and of course, he was not in good state with his superiors, who were all Nazis, anyway.
So when he wanted to get his job back, he needed some testimony and I explained to my first husband what had happened.
And he said, don't worry.
We're going to do something.

So he figured out something-- all in capital letter, with big stamps, American stuff, saying that Wolf Schieck had been an exemplary man, and that he had helped people, and that he should be reinstated.
And he was reinstated like that-- because of my husband who was a generous, kind man and understood me and wanted to make me happy.
So Wolf got his job back.
He didn't have much, because he came from Prussia, and Prussia was invaded by the Russian.
His wife and his daughter just made it in time to Gottingen, and that's where I went to see them.

But I can tell you then, when I was in the train going from Paris to Frankfurt, and then to Hanover near Gottingen, and I was in that train at night and I could hear the-- you know the track going boom-boom boom-boom.
I was thinking of the people in the cattle cars.
I could almost not bear it and that's why I never went back.
I went to see him, we spoke of all that, and we went to a restaurant where everything was written in Gothic letter.
And I remember it had-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], if you know German.
It means veal-- breast of veal.
And because of the writing of hinder, I read-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means the breast of children.
That's how my mind was.
I couldn't read anything that didn't speak to me of that.
So I didn't stay long.
I came back and I went back ever.
And I will never go back.
Why are you telling your story now?
Why you telling the whole experience?
Or did you agree--
Because I think I'm going to die in 10 years, maybe before, maybe, and then it will be gone, and people say it didn't happen.
That's why.
And I see Landsman did very well because he put testimonies, and all we saw-- well, It was about nine hours, but he has much, much, much, much more.
But also, my generation will disappear.
My children, mostly my older son will speak about it.
But after him, it's gone.
And it has to stay because I don't think anything like that ever happened.
Well, what impressed me is that when I could speak to a German, one, I was sure it would be OK, but if there were two, you could no longer speak.
This delation-- the people, that you couldn't trust anybody, that's what remains with me.
And that's when I went to the peace marches at the time of the Vietnam War.
I thought it was so marvelous that you would not be questioned, you would not be asked, you could do things, you could say, I don't like that war, I'm against it, and it was OK.
But there, you couldn't do anything.
Well, I wasn't breaking any--
Well, I felt it was not a law.
It was not a French law.
Well, at first, I told you, I was dumb.
I never-- I don't know, I don't have much imagination that way.
Oh, I'm rather--I'm too positive, always see the good result.
I don't see the bad one.

I remember at the time of the end of the war, I was near my grandmother, with my dad, and we were on a bicycle because I wanted to see my grandpa who was dying of cancer, and there was a bridge-- as it is in the Alps, very, very high.
An abbey is at the bottom.
And to go on that bridge it was written, anybody who steps on the bridge would immediately be shot.
OK.
I went on the bridge, I have a flat tire.
Of course my tire were not good.
So I come off my bike and I make a speech to the Germans down there.
They were with their machine gun.
I said, listen, I have a bad tire, I have a flat tire.
I have to walk.
They said, all right.
So when I went at the end of the bridge, two soldiers were there and they fixed my tire.
But you see?
Why it happened, I don't know, but that's how it was.
So I think I'm blessed some way.
[LAUGHS] We've come to the end of your story.
You have a pretty, little doll sitting right next to you there.
Could you-- you can pick her up.
Tell us a little bit about this.
Well, it is when Walter Tradelius came and he took me to the pastry shop.
I showed him my doll, and he thought they were not so good.
And he said that he would bring me one.
I don't know where he found it, but it is a German doll.
It comes from Nuremberg.
It's written on the neck here, made in Germany.
And he gave me that doll, whom even though, it's-- I call Crystal.
She's still Crystal.
And here she is.
She went through all my adventures, and came to America, and went back to France when I went back to France, came back here, went to live in Rhode Island and is in New York now.
But I have no granddaughter, so I don't know where Crystal will end.
Well, about 30, no, 60 years. 60 years, yeah. 60 years.
Probably after you're gone I'll think of many other things, but the whole thing, I remember, that was no sunshine for four years.
When I think of that time, I think in black and white.
But you have to say that until 1942, we did not know really.
We thought they were being resettled.
We knew it was bad, but we knew they would come back, and that would be an end to it.
But the camps, we didn't know.
The first I heard of it was through my friend Wolf Schieck.
He told me, behave yourself.
If something happens to you, there is nothing I can do.
And it is horrible.
That's when the first I heard-- he told me.
Well, they are very little.
This is a picture of me taken in 1943 at a friend's house, in the spring of 1943 after I-- the same year I buried Rita's mother.
Well, my friend gave it to me as a memory.
I have another one with her, but it is not a Jewish friend.
It is-- no.
No, just because we loved each other.
[LAUGHS]
This is a picture of my very good friend, almost sister, Rita Thalmann.
It is a picture taken when she was a school girl and she left it with me after her stay with my grandparents, just before she left for Switzerland.
Yes.
I brought it with me.
I kept it with my treasures.
This is a picture of the grave of the mother of Rita Thalmann, born Helen Haussman.
The grave was-- was changed.
The body was transported, you could say, or transferred from the potter's field where it had been laid during the war.
After the war, Rita and her brother arranged to have a mother, their mother, buried properly.
And it is still like this in Dijon, France now.
The Jewish cemetery, which is the same as the other cemetery, just it is an annex, but I never went to that grave.
I wished it be happier memories, but there are no happy memories of that time.
Thank you.
